 G-Tech Aguas Laboratory, Universidad de los Andes 
 
Company’s Business: 
Water research and treatment laboratory 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
March 13, 2026 
 

 
 
ABSTRACT 
G-Tech Aguas Laboratory, part of the Green Technologies Research Group at Universidad 
de los Andes, develops research activities focused on water treatment, water 
characterization, and microbiological processes for contaminant removal. During the 
pre-professional internship, work was carried out in two main areas. The first was the 
pilot-scale implementation of an electrocoagulation system for brine treatment, which 
included support in hydraulic reconditioning, system layout, preparation of electrodes, and 
testing of the module prior to operation. The second was the operation and monitoring of two 
biological reactors for nitrogen compound removal, including sampling, physicochemical 
analyses, data recording, and interpretation of reactor behavior. The most relevant challenge 
was the conditioning of the pilot-scale module, since it required understanding the hydraulic 
sequence of the system, solving operational problems, and supporting technical decisions 
needed to make the unit functional. The internship provided important learning in process 
analysis, laboratory work, planning, and engineering criteria. It also strengthened technical 
confidence, discipline in data handling, and the ability to understand and evaluate real 
treatment systems under experimental conditions.

1. INTRODUCTION 
The pre-professional internship was carried out from December 8, 2025, to March 5, 2026, 
at G-Tech Aguas Laboratory, located at Universidad de los Andes and linked to the Faculty 
of Engineering. The work was developed in a research environment focused on water 
treatment and characterization. The internship included activities related to pilot-scale brine 
treatment through alternating current electrocoagulation, a technique commonly applied in 
water and wastewater treatment (Mollah et al., 2001), and the monitoring of biological 
reactors for nitrogen compound removal. 
1.1 COMPANY DESCRIPTION 
G-Tech Aguas Laboratory was part of the Green Technologies Research Group at 
Universidad de los Andes and was linked to the Faculty of Engineering. The water treatment 
line led by Professor César Huilinir started in 2024, when the first activities were still carried 
out in the original G-Tech laboratory, located on the second floor of the Engineering 
Laboratory building. In March 2025, the new physical space was officially opened next to the 
Civil Works laboratory. From that moment, the work was organized in two adjacent sections: 
G-Tech Aguas and G-Tech Gases. 
As shown in Figure 1, G-Tech Aguas Laboratory operated in close connection with the 
original G-Tech laboratory and with G-Tech Gases. During the internship period, the 
laboratory had around eleven active members, including two professors, one laboratory 
coordinator, three PhD students, one thesis student, and five interns. The original G-Tech 
laboratory was mainly oriented toward bioprocesses, biological engineering, and chemical 
reactor studies, while G-Tech Aguas focused on water treatment and water characterization. 
G-Tech Gases was focused on projects involving microorganisms and gas-operated 
reactors. Although each laboratory developed its own projects autonomously, technical 
support was shared whenever needed. The laboratory also continued to receive new 
students through internships, thesis work, and graduate research activities. 
 
Figure 1. Diagram institutional location of G-Tech Aguas Laboratory. 
Source: Author’s own elaboration. 
3 

1.2 DEPARTMENT/AREA DESCRIPTION 
The internship was carried out in G-Tech Aguas Laboratory, specifically in the section 
dedicated to water-related projects. This area was part of a shared physical space located 
next to the Civil Works laboratory, where the facilities were divided between G-Tech Aguas 
and G-Tech Gases. The activities performed in this area included pilot-scale system 
implementation, hydraulic assembly support, sample collection, physicochemical analysis, 
biological reactor monitoring, culture media preparation, and data recording. The main 
responsibilities assigned during the internship were support in the implementation of a 
pilot-scale electrocoagulation system for brine treatment and, later, the monitoring, control, 
and characterization of two biological reactors for nitrogen compound removal. 
Academic supervision was provided by Professor César Huilinir Curío, who led the research 
line and supervised the general development of the projects. Daily supervision was mainly 
provided by Cristóbal Gallardo, who coordinated laboratory activities, managed operational 
resources, and supported the execution of the different projects. The closest working 
relationship was maintained with Cristóbal Gallardo, especially during the implementation 
and operation of the pilot-scale system. During the internship, the laboratory team also 
included PhD students, thesis students, and other interns working on different research 
activities. Limited but positive interaction was also maintained with nearby laboratories and 
university facilities, including the original G-Tech laboratory, the Civil Works laboratory, and 
other research spaces located in the same area of the campus. 
 
Figure 2. Diagram internal structure of G-Tech Aguas Laboratory during the internship. 
Source: Author’s own elaboration. 
 
4 

2. WORK DETAILS  
2.1 GENERAL ACTIVITIES 
During the internship, the general activities were developed in three main stages. First, a 
three-day training period was carried out, during which instruction was provided on the use 
of laboratory equipment, analytical methods, and general operating procedures. After this 
initial stage, support was provided to the implementation of a pilot-scale brine treatment 
system based on electrocoagulation. In the final stage of the internship, a second training 
period was carried out for the operation and monitoring of two biological reactors working 
with nitrifying and denitrifying microorganisms. 
Table 1: General Activities. 
Source: Author’s own elaboration.

The work related to the pilot-scale system began when the project moved from 
laboratory-scale research to pilot-scale implementation. Before this stage, the experimental 
work had already focused on the removal of ions from brine and on the possible use of the 
residues generated during treatment, which mainly corresponded to metallic ions dissolved 
in the aqueous stream. The internship student joined the project during this scale-up stage, 
when the main challenge was no longer the initial laboratory research, but the physical and 
operational implementation of the pilot unit. For this reason, the work was mainly oriented 
toward field engineering tasks such as equipment inspection, hydraulic assembly, cleaning 
of components, repair and adjustment of piping connections, preparation of aluminum 
electrodes, execution of hydraulic tests, and general conditioning of the module for future 
operation. This project was part of an externally funded applied research initiative developed 
by the laboratory for a company focused on commercializing innovative and environmentally 
sustainable treatment solutions capable of complying with environmental regulations. The 
implementation stage was carried out by Cristóbal Gallardo together with two interns, one of 
whom was the internship student. Interaction was also maintained with Gerardo, an 
Electrical Engineer from the associated company, who was responsible for the electrical 
aspects of the system and visited the laboratory approximately once a month. Weekly oral 
progress presentations were delivered by the internship student to Cristóbal Gallardo and 
Professor César Huilinir, and action plans were defined according to the needs of the 
following week. By the end of this stage, the remaining steps were the arrival and installation 
of the rectifier manufactured in China, the electrical connection of the aluminum cathodes 
and anodes, and the incorporation of the brine feed. 
During the last stage of the internship, support was provided to the operation and monitoring 
of two biological reactors for nitrogen compound removal. This work was initially part of a 
thesis project, and temporary support was required because the student in charge could not 
continue the operational follow-up until March. The main tasks included sampling, data 
recording, reactor control, and physicochemical analyses such as COD determination, nitrite 
and nitrate measurements by spectrophotometry, sulfate and TAN measurements by 
photometry, sulfide determination with electrodes, and monitoring of pH, alkalinity, and 
temperature with different probes. These activities required constant coordination with 
Cristóbal Gallardo and other members of the laboratory team. 
2.2 MOST CHALLENGING ACTIVITY 
The most challenging activity during the internship was the implementation and conditioning 
of the pilot-scale electrocoagulation module for brine treatment. Before this stage, the 
research had already been validated at laboratory scale, where ion removal from brine and 
6 

the possible use of the residues generated during treatment had been studied. After this 
validation stage, the project moved to pilot-scale implementation, which involved a significant 
increase in dimensions and operational complexity. In later stages, the project was expected 
to continue toward automation and future industrial-scale application. The internship student 
joined the project during this scale-up stage, when the main challenge was no longer the 
initial chemical research, but the physical, hydraulic, and operational implementation of the 
pilot unit. 
The module had been assigned by the associated company specifically for this project and 
was transported by truck to the university, where it was left next to the laboratory for its 
reconditioning and installation. The unit included four pumps in good condition, three tanks 
of approximately 1 m³ each, and one storage tank for brine of approximately 2 m³. From the 
storage tank, the brine was intended to be transferred to the treatment tanks where the 
process would take place. However, the hydraulic system and several of its connections 
were not in suitable condition for the new application. Some connections were damaged, 
while others were no longer useful for the intended process, so the system had to be 
restructured and reconditioned before pilot operation could begin. 
 
Figure 3. Repaired pipe of the feed section (indicated in orange box). 
Source: Author’s own elaboration. 
The first major challenge was understanding how the hydraulic system operated. This 
required learning how the tanks, pumps, valves, and pipe sections interacted, and how the 
sequence of operation affected fluid movement through the module. It was necessary to 
understand practical details such as the need to place ball valves in appropriate positions, 
the importance of isolating pumps for maintenance, and the suction conditions required for 
correct pump operation. One of the key aspects was that the liquid level in the tank could not 
7 

fall below certain conditions without affecting pump suction. This made it necessary to 
analyze the module as an integrated hydraulic system rather than as an isolated set of 
components. 
A second important challenge was the reconditioning of a module that had previously been 
used for a different purpose. Because of its previous use and transfer to the university, 
several parts of the system were not immediately suitable for pilot operation and had to be 
repaired, cleaned, or modified. The work included pump inspection and cleaning, repair of 
damaged hydraulic connections, installation of PVC lines and accessories, incorporation of 
union fittings to improve maintainability, cleaning of the treatment tank, and preparation of 
the aluminum plates for the treatment stage. One of the most representative modifications 
was the installation of a T-connection that allowed two discharge lines to be integrated into a 
single line, which simplified the system and avoided the use of an additional pump. A ball 
valve was also installed at the outlet of the precipitation tank to enable future sampling of 
solids generated during electrocoagulation. 
 
 
Figure 4. “T” joint and common line to drain, before and after (indicated in orange box). 
Source: Author’s own elaboration. 
Another major challenge appeared during the hydraulic testing stage. Once the module had 
been assembled, leakage points were detected in different parts of the system, including 
specific hoses and inlet connections. These leaks had to be corrected through sealing, 
adjustment, and practical field solutions until the hydraulic integrity of the module was 
acceptable. At the same time, filling and emptying tests were carried out in order to 
determine operating times and identify restrictions associated with height differences and 
pumping conditions. This stage was especially important because it showed that the pilot 
8 

unit could not be considered ready for operation until each connection and flow path had 
been verified under real conditions. 
 
Figure 5. Vanishing point, posterior condition (after). 
Source: Author’s own elaboration. 
A further challenge was related to the electrical stage of the project. The pilot module was 
being prepared to operate with a rectifier that had been ordered from China and had not yet 
arrived during the internship period. This equipment was required to generate the electrical 
conditions needed for electrocoagulation, including the connection of the aluminum cathodes 
and anodes and the application of controlled pulses. Because of this, the work carried out 
during the internship focused on leaving the module hydraulically and operationally prepared 
for the next commissioning stage. Support was also received from Gerardo, an Electrical 
Engineer from the associated company involved in the project, who visited periodically to 
review and improve the electrical part of the system. 
From the interpersonal and organizational point of view, this activity also required constant 
coordination and teamwork. The conditioning and implementation of the module were carried 
out mainly with the support of Cristóbal Gallardo and the other intern assigned to the project, 
with whom the internship student worked closely during assembly, adjustment, testing, and 
troubleshooting. Mutual support during this stage was essential, since the difficulties 
encountered had to be analyzed directly in the field and solved through discussion and 
practical testing. The perspective of Professor César Huilinir was important for defining 
criteria and reviewing progress, while the contributions of PhD and master’s students helped 
to clarify technical doubts and support decision-making. Weekly oral progress presentations 
were delivered by the internship student to Cristóbal Gallardo and Professor César Huilinir, 
9 

and these meetings were used to review progress, discuss problems, and define action 
plans for the following week. 
The results obtained by the end of this stage were relevant for the future operation of the 
pilot unit. The main difficulties identified during the conditioning process were analyzed, 
addressed, 
and 
resolved 
through 
successive 
adjustments, 
repairs, 
and design 
improvements. As a result, the module was left hydraulically and operationally prepared at 
the infrastructure level, with repaired and improved piping, better maintainability through 
union fittings, optimized discharge through the T-connection, sampling capacity through the 
ball valve, cleaned tanks, corrected leaks, and baseline filling and emptying data for future 
pilot campaigns. The remaining tasks were associated with the next commissioning stage 
and mainly included the arrival and installation of the rectifier, the final electrical connection 
of the aluminum plates, and the incorporation of the brine feed for operation under real 
process conditions. 
Figure 6. Perforations in aluminum plates. 
Source: Author’s own elaboration. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
10 

3. ANALYSIS 
3.1 COMPANY ANALYSIS  
G-Tech Aguas Laboratory operated as a small research laboratory with an estimated 
comfortable capacity of around twenty people. During the internship period, work schedules 
were flexible and depended on project needs. The laboratory coordinator usually worked 
from 9:00 a.m. to 6:00 p.m., interns from 10:00 a.m. to 5:00 p.m., and professors and 
graduate students attended according to the requirements of their experiments. 
From an organizational point of view, this flexible structure was effective because the 
laboratory worked with a small team and project-based tasks. Productivity depended mainly 
on planning, experimental timing, and completion of daily objectives, which made this 
dynamic appropriate for a research environment. 
Table 2: General Activities. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Source: Author’s own elaboration. 
A strength of the laboratory was its collaborative environment. Even though G-Tech Aguas, 
G-Tech Gases, and the original G-Tech laboratory worked on different projects under 
different coordinators and professors, mutual support was maintained whenever specific 
technical questions arose. This created a favorable working environment and allowed 
knowledge exchange between laboratories without affecting the autonomy of each project. 
At the same time, the laboratory showed growth potential, since new thesis students and 
possibly new master’s and PhD students were expected to join future projects. In that sense, 
the current structure was productive and functional, although future growth could require 
more formal planning of space, schedules, and equipment availability.

3.2 WORK ANALYSIS 
The work carried out during the internship can be analyzed through two main contributions: 
the pilot-scale implementation of an electrocoagulation system for brine treatment and the 
monitoring of two biological reactors for nitrogen compound removal. 
The first contribution was related to the pilot-scale electrocoagulation module for brine 
treatment. Electrocoagulation is a treatment technique in which sacrificial metal electrodes 
generate coagulant species in situ for contaminant removal from liquid streams (Mollah et 
al., 2001). In this project, the treatment concept had already been validated at laboratory 
scale, and the internship student joined the project during the pilot implementation stage, 
when the objective was to transfer the process to a larger scale and prepare it for future 
automation and possible industrial application. The associated company was also interested 
in the potential use of the recovered residues in other applications. 
As shown in Figure 7, the module was designed around four main hydraulic stages. First, 
brine was stored in a storage tank and transported by Pump 1 to the treatment tank, where 
the electrocoagulation process took place through the action of aluminum cathodes and 
anodes. The rectifier was intended to supply the electrical energy required for this stage and 
to allow polarity switching and pulsed operation. After approximately 40 minutes of 
treatment, the fluid was transferred by Pump 2 to the precipitation tank, where an external 
flocculant was added in order to improve solid-liquid separation and reduce settling time. 
Then, Pump 3 allowed the recirculation of the fluid from the precipitation tank back to the 
treatment tank, which gave flexibility to the operation and made it possible to continue the 
treatment cycle. Finally, Pump 4 was used to evacuate fluid from the precipitation and waste 
sections toward the drain. In addition, part of the solids generated during electrocoagulation 
were removed as foam, which overflowed through a bridge and was directed to the waste 
tank. 
 
Figure 7. Process diagram of the electrocoagulation module. 
Source: Author’s own elaboration. 
12 

From a work analysis perspective, the main contribution during this stage was understanding 
the operating sequence of the module and supporting the hydraulic arrangement required to 
make it functional at pilot scale. This included planning valves, unions, pumping lines, and 
hydraulic connections so that the system could operate in a practical and maintainable way. 
As described in Section 2.2, this stage also required several modifications, such as 
reconditioning damaged connections and improving the discharge arrangement. 
Another important point was the future operation of the system. During the internship, the 
rectifier had not yet arrived, so the module could not be tested under full electrocoagulation 
conditions, although it was left prepared for the next commissioning stage. This was relevant 
because pulsed or alternating current can reduce electrode passivation in aluminum-based 
systems (Mao et al., 2008). However, the use of aluminum electrodes also implies material 
consumption and generation of metallic residues, which may become a limitation in future 
automated or industrial applications. For this reason, alternative electrode materials were 
already being considered. 
The second contribution corresponded to the monitoring of two biological reactors. In this 
project, the main objective was to evaluate whether nitrifying and denitrifying 
microorganisms could sustain both processes in the same system with limited direct 
aeration, using passive oxygen transfer through a perforated upper mesh (Thakur & Medhi, 
2019). At the same time, the effect of zeolite was assessed by comparing a control reactor 
and a reactor containing zeolite. 
  
 
Figure 8. Evolution of COD in the control reactor and the zeolite reactor during a representative 
operating cycle. 
Source: Author’s own elaboration. 
Samples were taken on Mondays, Wednesdays, and Fridays, with additional sampling on 
Wednesdays due to media replacement. After the initial learning stage, the work focused on 
routine monitoring, data recording, and analysis. The monitored variables included COD, 
TAN, nitrite, nitrate, sulfate, sulfide, pH, alkalinity, and temperature. COD was especially 
relevant because it represented the organic load available in the medium, and its decrease 
13 

in both reactors suggested partial substrate consumption. The internship student monitored 
the reactors from cycle 24 to cycle 27, and the overall behavior was similar in all of them. For 
this reason, only one representative cycle is shown in the figures. Accordingly, Figure 8 
should present the decrease in COD in both reactors during a representative cycle. 
At the same time, the nitrogen-related variables were essential for evaluating whether 
nitrification and denitrification were actually taking place under the tested conditions. TAN 
decreased during the cycle, which was consistent with biological activity in the system. 
However, nitrite and nitrate were not clearly produced, so the expected nitrification pathway 
could not be confirmed from the analytical results. As a consequence, denitrification could 
not be clearly demonstrated either, since nitrate or nitrite were not sufficiently available as 
intermediates for further reduction. As in the COD analysis, the internship student worked 
from cycle 24 to cycle 27 and similar trends were observed in all of them. Therefore, Figure 
9 should present the evolution of TAN together with the absence or very low generation of 
nitrite and nitrate in both reactors during one representative cycle. 
     
 
Figure 9. TAN, nitrite, and nitrate evolution in the control reactor and the zeolite reactor during a 
representative operating cycle. 
Source: Author’s own elaboration. 
By the end of February, the tested conditions did not clearly demonstrate simultaneous 
nitrification and denitrification. COD and TAN decreased, indicating biological activity, but the 
absence of clear nitrite and nitrate production meant that the nitrogen pathway could not be 
fully confirmed. In contrast, sulfide reduction was observed. From a work analysis 
perspective, this stage was mainly based on reliable operation, routine monitoring, and 
interpretation of experimental behavior. The results also suggested that a more controlled 
aeration strategy could improve process predictability in future work. 
 
 
14 

3. PERSONAL REFLECTION 
This internship represented an important stage in both my professional and personal 
development. It allowed me to understand my own value with greater clarity and to identify 
more precisely my strengths, weaknesses, and distinguishing qualities. One of the main 
lessons obtained from this experience was the confirmation that I work best when I 
understand a process in depth. I tend to analyze what I am doing carefully, investigate the 
technical basis of each activity, and try to anticipate the possible behavior of the system. 
This approach not only improved my own performance, but also helped build trust in the 
work assigned to me. At the same time, the experience reinforced the importance of 
planning and organization as fundamental elements in engineering work. 
 
From a technical perspective, the internship allowed me to strengthen my understanding of 
chemistry, process behavior, and microbiological systems under real laboratory conditions. 
My contribution to the laboratory was mainly based on daily support, careful execution of 
assigned tasks, and responsible handling of samples and data. I aimed to perform each 
activity with concentration, consistency, and the lowest possible level of experimental error. 
In return, the laboratory provided an environment in which learning was progressive and well 
supported. Working with experienced and collaborative people helped me clarify doubts, 
improve my technical judgment, and better understand the human side of professional work. 
This experience also helped me mature, especially by understanding that mistakes are part 
of the learning process and that they should be approached with responsibility and a 
willingness to improve. After this internship, I continue the next stage of my academic path 
with greater confidence, stronger motivation, and a clearer understanding of my professional 
interests. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
15

ABSTRACT 
G-Tech Aguas Laboratory, part of the Green Technologies Research Group at Universidad 
de los Andes, develops research activities focused on water treatment, water 
characterization, and microbiological processes for contaminant removal. During the 
pre-professional internship, work was carried out in two main areas. The first was the 
pilot-scale implementation of an electrocoagulation system for brine treatment, which 
included support in hydraulic reconditioning, system layout, preparation of electrodes, and

analyses, data recording, and interpretation of reactor behavior. The most relevant challenge 
was the conditioning of the pilot-scale module, since it required understanding the hydraulic

2.1 GENERAL ACTIVITIES 
During the internship, the general activities were developed in three main stages. First, a 
three-day training period was carried out, during which instruction was provided on the use 
of laboratory equipment, analytical methods, and general operating procedures. After this 
initial stage, support was provided to the implementation of a pilot-scale brine treatment 
system based on electrocoagulation. In the final stage of the internship, a second training 
period was carried out for the operation and monitoring of two biological reactors working 
with nitrifying and denitrifying microorganisms.

The work related to the pilot-scale system began when the project moved from 
laboratory-scale research to pilot-scale implementation. Before this stage, the experimental 
work had already focused on the removal of ions from brine and on the possible use of the 
residues generated during treatment, which mainly corresponded to metallic ions dissolved 
in the aqueous stream. The internship student joined the project during this scale-up stage, 
when the main challenge was no longer the initial laboratory research, but the physical and 
operational implementation of the pilot unit. For this reason, the work was mainly oriented 
toward field engineering tasks such as equipment inspection, hydraulic assembly, cleaning 
of components, repair and adjustment of piping connections, preparation of aluminum 
electrodes, execution of hydraulic tests, and general conditioning of the module for future 
operation. This project was part of an externally funded applied research initiative developed 
by the laboratory for a company focused on commercializing innovative and environmentally 
sustainable treatment solutions capable of complying with environmental regulations. The 
implementation stage was carried out by Cristóbal Gallardo together with two interns, one of 
whom was the internship student. Interaction was also maintained with Gerardo, an 
Electrical Engineer from the associated company, who was responsible for the electrical 
aspects of the system and visited the laboratory approximately once a month. Weekly oral 
progress presentations were delivered by the internship student to Cristóbal Gallardo and 
Professor César Huilinir, and action plans were defined according to the needs of the 
following week. By the end of this stage, the remaining steps were the arrival and installation 
of the rectifier manufactured in China, the electrical connection of the aluminum cathodes 
and anodes, and the incorporation of the brine feed. 
During the last stage of the internship, support was provided to the operation and monitoring 
of two biological reactors for nitrogen compound removal. This work was initially part of a 
thesis project, and temporary support was required because the student in charge could not 
continue the operational follow-up until March. The main tasks included sampling, data 
recording, reactor control, and physicochemical analyses such as COD determination, nitrite 
and nitrate measurements by spectrophotometry, sulfate and TAN measurements by 
photometry, sulfide determination with electrodes, and monitoring of pH, alkalinity, and 
temperature with different probes. These activities required constant coordination with 
Cristóbal Gallardo and other members of the laboratory team.

most_challenge_activity.texto:
2.2 MOST CHALLENGING ACTIVITY 
The most challenging activity during the internship was the implementation and conditioning 
of the pilot-scale electrocoagulation module for brine treatment. Before this stage, the 
research had already been validated at laboratory scale, where ion removal from brine and 
6 

the possible use of the residues generated during treatment had been studied. After this 
validation stage, the project moved to pilot-scale implementation, which involved a significant 
increase in dimensions and operational complexity. In later stages, the project was expected 
to continue toward automation and future industrial-scale application. The internship student 
joined the project during this scale-up stage, when the main challenge was no longer the 
initial chemical research, but the physical, hydraulic, and operational implementation of the 
pilot unit. 
The module had been assigned by the associated company specifically for this project and 
was transported by truck to the university, where it was left next to the laboratory for its 
reconditioning and installation. The unit included four pumps in good condition, three tanks 
of approximately 1 m³ each, and one storage tank for brine of approximately 2 m³. From the 
storage tank, the brine was intended to be transferred to the treatment tanks where the 
process would take place. However, the hydraulic system and several of its connections 
were not in suitable condition for the new application. Some connections were damaged, 
while others were no longer useful for the intended process, so the system had to be 
restructured and reconditioned before pilot operation could begin. 
 
Figure 3. Repaired pipe of the feed section (indicated in orange box). 
Source: Author’s own elaboration. 
The first major challenge was understanding how the hydraulic system operated. This 
required learning how the tanks, pumps, valves, and pipe sections interacted, and how the 
sequence of operation affected fluid movement through the module. It was necessary to 
understand practical details such as the need to place ball valves in appropriate positions, 
the importance of isolating pumps for maintenance, and the suction conditions required for 
correct pump operation. One of the key aspects was that the liquid level in the tank could not 
7 

fall below certain conditions without affecting pump suction. This made it necessary to 
analyze the module as an integrated hydraulic system rather than as an isolated set of 
components. 
A second important challenge was the reconditioning of a module that had previously been 
used for a different purpose. Because of its previous use and transfer to the university, 
several parts of the system were not immediately suitable for pilot operation and had to be 
repaired, cleaned, or modified. The work included pump inspection and cleaning, repair of 
damaged hydraulic connections, installation of PVC lines and accessories, incorporation of 
union fittings to improve maintainability, cleaning of the treatment tank, and preparation of 
the aluminum plates for the treatment stage. One of the most representative modifications 
was the installation of a T-connection that allowed two discharge lines to be integrated into a 
single line, which simplified the system and avoided the use of an additional pump. A ball 
valve was also installed at the outlet of the precipitation tank to enable future sampling of 
solids generated during electrocoagulation. 
 
 
Figure 4. “T” joint and common line to drain, before and after (indicated in orange box). 
Source: Author’s own elaboration. 
Another major challenge appeared during the hydraulic testing stage. Once the module had 
been assembled, leakage points were detected in different parts of the system, including 
specific hoses and inlet connections. These leaks had to be corrected through sealing, 
adjustment, and practical field solutions until the hydraulic integrity of the module was 
acceptable. At the same time, filling and emptying tests were carried out in order to 
determine operating times and identify restrictions associated with height differences and 
pumping conditions. This stage was especially important because it showed that the pilot 
8 

unit could not be considered ready for operation until each connection and flow path had 
been verified under real conditions. 
 
Figure 5. Vanishing point, posterior condition (after). 
Source: Author’s own elaboration. 
A further challenge was related to the electrical stage of the project. The pilot module was 
being prepared to operate with a rectifier that had been ordered from China and had not yet 
arrived during the internship period. This equipment was required to generate the electrical 
conditions needed for electrocoagulation, including the connection of the aluminum cathodes 
and anodes and the application of controlled pulses. Because of this, the work carried out 
during the internship focused on leaving the module hydraulically and operationally prepared 
for the next commissioning stage. Support was also received from Gerardo, an Electrical 
Engineer from the associated company involved in the project, who visited periodically to 
review and improve the electrical part of the system. 
From the interpersonal and organizational point of view, this activity also required constant 
coordination and teamwork. The conditioning and implementation of the module were carried 
out mainly with the support of Cristóbal Gallardo and the other intern assigned to the project, 
with whom the internship student worked closely during assembly, adjustment, testing, and 
troubleshooting. Mutual support during this stage was essential, since the difficulties 
encountered had to be analyzed directly in the field and solved through discussion and 
practical testing. The perspective of Professor César Huilinir was important for defining 
criteria and reviewing progress, while the contributions of PhD and master’s students helped 
to clarify technical doubts and support decision-making. Weekly oral progress presentations 
were delivered by the internship student to Cristóbal Gallardo and Professor César Huilinir, 
9 

and these meetings were used to review progress, discuss problems, and define action 
plans for the following week. 
The results obtained by the end of this stage were relevant for the future operation of the 
pilot unit. The main difficulties identified during the conditioning process were analyzed, 
addressed, 
and 
resolved 
through 
successive 
adjustments, 
repairs, 
and design 
improvements. As a result, the module was left hydraulically and operationally prepared at 
the infrastructure level, with repaired and improved piping, better maintainability through 
union fittings, optimized discharge through the T-connection, sampling capacity through the 
ball valve, cleaned tanks, corrected leaks, and baseline filling and emptying data for future 
pilot campaigns. The remaining tasks were associated with the next commissioning stage 
and mainly included the arrival and installation of the rectifier, the final electrical connection 
of the aluminum plates, and the incorporation of the brine feed for operation under real 
process conditions. 
Figure 6. Perforations in aluminum plates. 
Source: Author’s own elaboration. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
10 

3. ANALYSIS

company_analysis.texto:
3.1 COMPANY ANALYSIS  
G-Tech Aguas Laboratory operated as a small research laboratory with an estimated 
comfortable capacity of around twenty people. During the internship period, work schedules 
were flexible and depended on project needs. The laboratory coordinator usually worked 
from 9:00 a.m. to 6:00 p.m., interns from 10:00 a.m. to 5:00 p.m., and professors and 
graduate students attended according to the requirements of their experiments. 
From an organizational point of view, this flexible structure was effective because the 
laboratory worked with a small team and project-based tasks. Productivity depended mainly 
on planning, experimental timing, and completion of daily objectives, which made this 
dynamic appropriate for a research environment. 
Table 2: General Activities. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Source: Author’s own elaboration. 
A strength of the laboratory was its collaborative environment. Even though G-Tech Aguas, 
G-Tech Gases, and the original G-Tech laboratory worked on different projects under 
different coordinators and professors, mutual support was maintained whenever specific 
technical questions arose. This created a favorable working environment and allowed 
knowledge exchange between laboratories without affecting the autonomy of each project. 
At the same time, the laboratory showed growth potential, since new thesis students and 
possibly new master’s and PhD students were expected to join future projects. In that sense, 
the current structure was productive and functional, although future growth could require 
more formal planning of space, schedules, and equipment availability.

work_analysis.texto:
3.2 WORK ANALYSIS 
The work carried out during the internship can be analyzed through two main contributions: 
the pilot-scale implementation of an electrocoagulation system for brine treatment and the 
monitoring of two biological reactors for nitrogen compound removal. 
The first contribution was related to the pilot-scale electrocoagulation module for brine 
treatment. Electrocoagulation is a treatment technique in which sacrificial metal electrodes 
generate coagulant species in situ for contaminant removal from liquid streams (Mollah et 
al., 2001). In this project, the treatment concept had already been validated at laboratory 
scale, and the internship student joined the project during the pilot implementation stage, 
when the objective was to transfer the process to a larger scale and prepare it for future 
automation and possible industrial application. The associated company was also interested 
in the potential use of the recovered residues in other applications. 
As shown in Figure 7, the module was designed around four main hydraulic stages. First, 
brine was stored in a storage tank and transported by Pump 1 to the treatment tank, where 
the electrocoagulation process took place through the action of aluminum cathodes and 
anodes. The rectifier was intended to supply the electrical energy required for this stage and 
to allow polarity switching and pulsed operation. After approximately 40 minutes of 
treatment, the fluid was transferred by Pump 2 to the precipitation tank, where an external 
flocculant was added in order to improve solid-liquid separation and reduce settling time. 
Then, Pump 3 allowed the recirculation of the fluid from the precipitation tank back to the 
treatment tank, which gave flexibility to the operation and made it possible to continue the 
treatment cycle. Finally, Pump 4 was used to evacuate fluid from the precipitation and waste 
sections toward the drain. In addition, part of the solids generated during electrocoagulation 
were removed as foam, which overflowed through a bridge and was directed to the waste 
tank. 
 
Figure 7. Process diagram of the electrocoagulation module. 
Source: Author’s own elaboration. 
12 

From a work analysis perspective, the main contribution during this stage was understanding 
the operating sequence of the module and supporting the hydraulic arrangement required to 
make it functional at pilot scale. This included planning valves, unions, pumping lines, and 
hydraulic connections so that the system could operate in a practical and maintainable way. 
As described in Section 2.2, this stage also required several modifications, such as 
reconditioning damaged connections and improving the discharge arrangement. 
Another important point was the future operation of the system. During the internship, the 
rectifier had not yet arrived, so the module could not be tested under full electrocoagulation 
conditions, although it was left prepared for the next commissioning stage. This was relevant 
because pulsed or alternating current can reduce electrode passivation in aluminum-based 
systems (Mao et al., 2008). However, the use of aluminum electrodes also implies material 
consumption and generation of metallic residues, which may become a limitation in future 
automated or industrial applications. For this reason, alternative electrode materials were 
already being considered. 
The second contribution corresponded to the monitoring of two biological reactors. In this 
project, the main objective was to evaluate whether nitrifying and denitrifying 
microorganisms could sustain both processes in the same system with limited direct 
aeration, using passive oxygen transfer through a perforated upper mesh (Thakur & Medhi, 
2019). At the same time, the effect of zeolite was assessed by comparing a control reactor 
and a reactor containing zeolite. 
  
 
Figure 8. Evolution of COD in the control reactor and the zeolite reactor during a representative 
operating cycle. 
Source: Author’s own elaboration. 
Samples were taken on Mondays, Wednesdays, and Fridays, with additional sampling on 
Wednesdays due to media replacement. After the initial learning stage, the work focused on 
routine monitoring, data recording, and analysis. The monitored variables included COD, 
TAN, nitrite, nitrate, sulfate, sulfide, pH, alkalinity, and temperature. COD was especially 
relevant because it represented the organic load available in the medium, and its decrease 
13 

in both reactors suggested partial substrate consumption. The internship student monitored 
the reactors from cycle 24 to cycle 27, and the overall behavior was similar in all of them. For 
this reason, only one representative cycle is shown in the figures. Accordingly, Figure 8 
should present the decrease in COD in both reactors during a representative cycle. 
At the same time, the nitrogen-related variables were essential for evaluating whether 
nitrification and denitrification were actually taking place under the tested conditions. TAN 
decreased during the cycle, which was consistent with biological activity in the system. 
However, nitrite and nitrate were not clearly produced, so the expected nitrification pathway 
could not be confirmed from the analytical results. As a consequence, denitrification could 
not be clearly demonstrated either, since nitrate or nitrite were not sufficiently available as 
intermediates for further reduction. As in the COD analysis, the internship student worked 
from cycle 24 to cycle 27 and similar trends were observed in all of them. Therefore, Figure 
9 should present the evolution of TAN together with the absence or very low generation of 
nitrite and nitrate in both reactors during one representative cycle. 
     
 
Figure 9. TAN, nitrite, and nitrate evolution in the control reactor and the zeolite reactor during a 
representative operating cycle. 
Source: Author’s own elaboration. 
By the end of February, the tested conditions did not clearly demonstrate simultaneous 
nitrification and denitrification. COD and TAN decreased, indicating biological activity, but the 
absence of clear nitrite and nitrate production meant that the nitrogen pathway could not be 
fully confirmed. In contrast, sulfide reduction was observed. From a work analysis 
perspective, this stage was mainly based on reliable operation, routine monitoring, and 
interpretation of experimental behavior. The results also suggested that a more controlled 
aeration strategy could improve process predictability in future work. 
 
 
14 

3. PERSONAL REFLECTION 
This internship represented an important stage in both my professional and personal 
development. It allowed me to understand my own value with greater clarity and to identify 
more precisely my strengths, weaknesses, and distinguishing qualities. One of the main 
lessons obtained from this experience was the confirmation that I work best when I 
understand a process in depth. I tend to analyze what I am doing carefully, investigate the 
technical basis of each activity, and try to anticipate the possible behavior of the system. 
This approach not only improved my own performance, but also helped build trust in the 
work assigned to me. At the same time, the experience reinforced the importance of 
planning and organization as fundamental elements in engineering work. 
 
From a technical perspective, the internship allowed me to strengthen my understanding of 
chemistry, process behavior, and microbiological systems under real laboratory conditions. 
My contribution to the laboratory was mainly based on daily support, careful execution of 
assigned tasks, and responsible handling of samples and data. I aimed to perform each 
activity with concentration, consistency, and the lowest possible level of experimental error. 
In return, the laboratory provided an environment in which learning was progressive and well 
supported. Working with experienced and collaborative people helped me clarify doubts, 
improve my technical judgment, and better understand the human side of professional work. 
This experience also helped me mature, especially by understanding that mistakes are part 
of the learning process and that they should be approached with responsibility and a 
willingness to improve. After this internship, I continue the next stage of my academic path 
with greater confidence, stronger motivation, and a clearer understanding of my professional 
interests.

For this reason, the work was mainly oriented  toward field engineering tasks such as equipment inspection, hydraulic assembly, cleaning  of components, repair and adjustment of piping connections, preparation of aluminum  electrodes, execution of hydraulic tests, and general conditioning of the module for future  operation.

This project was part of an externally funded applied research initiative developed  by the laboratory for a company focused on commercializing innovative and environmentally  sustainable treatment solutions capable of complying with environmental regulations. The  implementation stage was carried out by Cristóbal Gallardo together with two interns, one of  whom was the internship student.

Interaction was also maintained with Gerardo, an  Electrical Engineer from the associated company, who was responsible for the electrical  aspects of the system and visited the laboratory approximately once a month. Weekly oral  progress presentations were delivered by the internship student to Cristóbal Gallardo and  Professor César Huilinir, and action plans were defined according to the needs of the  following week.

By the end of this stage, the remaining steps were the arrival and installation  of the rectifier manufactured in China, the electrical connection of the aluminum cathodes  and anodes, and the incorporation of the brine feed. During the last stage of the internship, support was provided to the operation and monitoring  of two biological reactors for nitrogen compound removal.
This work was initially part of a  thesis project, and temporary support was required because the student in charge could not  continue the operational follow-up until March.

The main tasks included sampling, data  recording, reactor control, and physicochemical analyses such as COD determination, nitrite  and nitrate measurements by spectrophotometry, sulfate and TAN measurements by  photometry, sulfide determination with electrodes, and monitoring of pH, alkalinity, and  temperature with different probes. These activities required constant coordination with  Cristóbal Gallardo and other members of the laboratory team.

2.2 MOST CHALLENGING ACTIVITY  The most challenging activity during the internship was the implementation and conditioning  of the pilot-scale electrocoagulation module for brine treatment. Before this stage, the  research had already been validated at laboratory scale, where ion removal from brine and  6   the possible use of the residues generated during treatment had been studied.

After this  validation stage, the project moved to pilot-scale implementation, which involved a significant  increase in dimensions and operational complexity. In later stages, the project was expected  to continue toward automation and future industrial-scale application. The internship student  joined the project during this scale-up stage, when the main challenge was no longer the  initial chemical research, but the physical, hydraulic, and operational implementation of the  pilot unit.

The module had been assigned by the associated company specifically for this project and  was transported by truck to the university, where it was left next to the laboratory for its  reconditioning and installation. The unit included four pumps in good condition, three tanks  of approximately 1 m³ each, and one storage tank for brine of approximately 2 m³. From the  storage tank, the brine was intended to be transferred to the treatment tanks where the  process would take place.

However, the hydraulic system and several of its connections  were not in suitable condition for the new application. Some connections were damaged,  while others were no longer useful for the intended process, so the system had to be  restructured and reconditioned before pilot operation could begin. Figure 3. Repaired pipe of the feed section (indicated in orange box). Source: Author’s own elaboration. The first major challenge was understanding how the hydraulic system operated.

This  required learning how the tanks, pumps, valves, and pipe sections interacted, and how the  sequence of operation affected fluid movement through the module. It was necessary to  understand practical details such as the need to place ball valves in appropriate positions,  the importance of isolating pumps for maintenance, and the suction conditions required for  correct pump operation.

One of the key aspects was that the liquid level in the tank could not  7   fall below certain conditions without affecting pump suction. This made it necessary to  analyze the module as an integrated hydraulic system rather than as an isolated set of  components. A second important challenge was the reconditioning of a module that had previously been  used for a different purpose.

Because of its previous use and transfer to the university,  several parts of the system were not immediately suitable for pilot operation and had to be  repaired, cleaned, or modified. The work included pump inspection and cleaning, repair of  damaged hydraulic connections, installation of PVC lines and accessories, incorporation of  union fittings to improve maintainability, cleaning of the treatment tank, and preparation of  the aluminum plates for the treatment stage.

One of the most representative modifications  was the installation of a T-connection that allowed two discharge lines to be integrated into a  single line, which simplified the system and avoided the use of an additional pump. A ball  valve was also installed at the outlet of the precipitation tank to enable future sampling of  solids generated during electrocoagulation. Figure 4. “T” joint and common line to drain, before and after (indicated in orange box). Source: Author’s own elaboration.

Another major challenge appeared during the hydraulic testing stage. Once the module had  been assembled, leakage points were detected in different parts of the system, including  specific hoses and inlet connections. These leaks had to be corrected through sealing,  adjustment, and practical field solutions until the hydraulic integrity of the module was  acceptable.

At the same time, filling and emptying tests were carried out in order to  determine operating times and identify restrictions associated with height differences and  pumping conditions. This stage was especially important because it showed that the pilot  8   unit could not be considered ready for operation until each connection and flow path had  been verified under real conditions. Figure 5. Vanishing point, posterior condition (after). Source: Author’s own elaboration.

A further challenge was related to the electrical stage of the project. The pilot module was  being prepared to operate with a rectifier that had been ordered from China and had not yet  arrived during the internship period. This equipment was required to generate the electrical  conditions needed for electrocoagulation, including the connection of the aluminum cathodes  and anodes and the application of controlled pulses.

Because of this, the work carried out  during the internship focused on leaving the module hydraulically and operationally prepared  for the next commissioning stage. Support was also received from Gerardo, an Electrical  Engineer from the associated company involved in the project, who visited periodically to  review and improve the electrical part of the system. From the interpersonal and organizational point of view, this activity also required constant  coordination and teamwork.

The conditioning and implementation of the module were carried  out mainly with the support of Cristóbal Gallardo and the other intern assigned to the project,  with whom the internship student worked closely during assembly, adjustment, testing, and  troubleshooting. Mutual support during this stage was essential, since the difficulties  encountered had to be analyzed directly in the field and solved through discussion and  practical testing.

The perspective of Professor César Huilinir was important for defining  criteria and reviewing progress, while the contributions of PhD and master’s students helped  to clarify technical doubts and support decision-making. Weekly oral progress presentations  were delivered by the internship student to Cristóbal Gallardo and Professor César Huilinir,  9   and these meetings were used to review progress, discuss problems, and define action  plans for the following week.

The results obtained by the end of this stage were relevant for the future operation of the  pilot unit. The main difficulties identified during the conditioning process were analyzed,  addressed,  and  resolved  through  successive  adjustments,  repairs,  and design  improvements.

As a result, the module was left hydraulically and operationally prepared at  the infrastructure level, with repaired and improved piping, better maintainability through  union fittings, optimized discharge through the T-connection, sampling capacity through the  ball valve, cleaned tanks, corrected leaks, and baseline filling and emptying data for future  pilot campaigns.

The remaining tasks were associated with the next commissioning stage  and mainly included the arrival and installation of the rectifier, the final electrical connection  of the aluminum plates, and the incorporation of the brine feed for operation under real  process conditions. Figure 6. Perforations in aluminum plates. Source: Author’s own elaboration. 10   3. ANALYSIS.

3.1 COMPANY ANALYSIS   G-Tech Aguas Laboratory operated as a small research laboratory with an estimated  comfortable capacity of around twenty people. During the internship period, work schedules  were flexible and depended on project needs. The laboratory coordinator usually worked  from 9:00 a.m. to 6:00 p.m., interns from 10:00 a.m. to 5:00 p.m., and professors and  graduate students attended according to the requirements of their experiments.

From an organizational point of view, this flexible structure was effective because the  laboratory worked with a small team and project-based tasks. Productivity depended mainly  on planning, experimental timing, and completion of daily objectives, which made this  dynamic appropriate for a research environment. Table 2: General Activities. Source: Author’s own elaboration. A strength of the laboratory was its collaborative environment.

Even though G-Tech Aguas,  G-Tech Gases, and the original G-Tech laboratory worked on different projects under  different coordinators and professors, mutual support was maintained whenever specific  technical questions arose. This created a favorable working environment and allowed  knowledge exchange between laboratories without affecting the autonomy of each project.

At the same time, the laboratory showed growth potential, since new thesis students and  possibly new master’s and PhD students were expected to join future projects. In that sense,  the current structure was productive and functional, although future growth could require  more formal planning of space, schedules, and equipment availability. 11  Aspect  Observation  Estimated comfortable  capacity  Around 20 people  Laboratory coordinator  schedule  9:00 a.m. – 6:00 p.m.

Intern schedule  10:00 a.m. – 5:00 p.m. Professors / graduate  students  Flexible attendance depending on project needs  Main organizational  characteristic  Flexible, project-based work dynamic  Main strength  High productivity and collaboration  Main future challenge  Space and resource planning as the group grows.

3.2 WORK ANALYSIS  The work carried out during the internship can be analyzed through two main contributions:  the pilot-scale implementation of an electrocoagulation system for brine treatment and the  monitoring of two biological reactors for nitrogen compound removal. The first contribution was related to the pilot-scale electrocoagulation module for brine  treatment.

Electrocoagulation is a treatment technique in which sacrificial metal electrodes  generate coagulant species in situ for contaminant removal from liquid streams (Mollah et  al., 2001). In this project, the treatment concept had already been validated at laboratory  scale, and the internship student joined the project during the pilot implementation stage,  when the objective was to transfer the process to a larger scale and prepare it for future  automation and possible industrial application.

The associated company was also interested  in the potential use of the recovered residues in other applications. As shown in Figure 7, the module was designed around four main hydraulic stages. First,  brine was stored in a storage tank and transported by Pump 1 to the treatment tank, where  the electrocoagulation process took place through the action of aluminum cathodes and  anodes.

The rectifier was intended to supply the electrical energy required for this stage and  to allow polarity switching and pulsed operation. After approximately 40 minutes of  treatment, the fluid was transferred by Pump 2 to the precipitation tank, where an external  flocculant was added in order to improve solid-liquid separation and reduce settling time.

Then, Pump 3 allowed the recirculation of the fluid from the precipitation tank back to the  treatment tank, which gave flexibility to the operation and made it possible to continue the  treatment cycle. Finally, Pump 4 was used to evacuate fluid from the precipitation and waste  sections toward the drain. In addition, part of the solids generated during electrocoagulation  were removed as foam, which overflowed through a bridge and was directed to the waste  tank. Figure 7.

Process diagram of the electrocoagulation module. Source: Author’s own elaboration. 12   From a work analysis perspective, the main contribution during this stage was understanding  the operating sequence of the module and supporting the hydraulic arrangement required to  make it functional at pilot scale. This included planning valves, unions, pumping lines, and  hydraulic connections so that the system could operate in a practical and maintainable way.

As described in Section 2.2, this stage also required several modifications, such as  reconditioning damaged connections and improving the discharge arrangement. Another important point was the future operation of the system. During the internship, the  rectifier had not yet arrived, so the module could not be tested under full electrocoagulation  conditions, although it was left prepared for the next commissioning stage.

This was relevant  because pulsed or alternating current can reduce electrode passivation in aluminum-based  systems (Mao et al., 2008). However, the use of aluminum electrodes also implies material  consumption and generation of metallic residues, which may become a limitation in future  automated or industrial applications. For this reason, alternative electrode materials were  already being considered. The second contribution corresponded to the monitoring of two biological reactors.

In this  project, the main objective was to evaluate whether nitrifying and denitrifying  microorganisms could sustain both processes in the same system with limited direct  aeration, using passive oxygen transfer through a perforated upper mesh (Thakur & Medhi,  2019). At the same time, the effect of zeolite was assessed by comparing a control reactor  and a reactor containing zeolite. Figure 8.

Evolution of COD in the control reactor and the zeolite reactor during a representative  operating cycle. Source: Author’s own elaboration. Samples were taken on Mondays, Wednesdays, and Fridays, with additional sampling on  Wednesdays due to media replacement. After the initial learning stage, the work focused on  routine monitoring, data recording, and analysis. The monitored variables included COD,  TAN, nitrite, nitrate, sulfate, sulfide, pH, alkalinity, and temperature.

COD was especially  relevant because it represented the organic load available in the medium, and its decrease  13   in both reactors suggested partial substrate consumption. The internship student monitored  the reactors from cycle 24 to cycle 27, and the overall behavior was similar in all of them. For  this reason, only one representative cycle is shown in the figures. Accordingly, Figure 8  should present the decrease in COD in both reactors during a representative cycle.

At the same time, the nitrogen-related variables were essential for evaluating whether  nitrification and denitrification were actually taking place under the tested conditions. TAN  decreased during the cycle, which was consistent with biological activity in the system. However, nitrite and nitrate were not clearly produced, so the expected nitrification pathway  could not be confirmed from the analytical results.

As a consequence, denitrification could  not be clearly demonstrated either, since nitrate or nitrite were not sufficiently available as  intermediates for further reduction. As in the COD analysis, the internship student worked  from cycle 24 to cycle 27 and similar trends were observed in all of them. Therefore, Figure  9 should present the evolution of TAN together with the absence or very low generation of  nitrite and nitrate in both reactors during one representative cycle. Figure 9.

TAN, nitrite, and nitrate evolution in the control reactor and the zeolite reactor during a  representative operating cycle. Source: Author’s own elaboration. By the end of February, the tested conditions did not clearly demonstrate simultaneous  nitrification and denitrification. COD and TAN decreased, indicating biological activity, but the  absence of clear nitrite and nitrate production meant that the nitrogen pathway could not be  fully confirmed.

In contrast, sulfide reduction was observed. From a work analysis  perspective, this stage was mainly based on reliable operation, routine monitoring, and  interpretation of experimental behavior. The results also suggested that a more controlled  aeration strategy could improve process predictability in future work. 14   3. PERSONAL REFLECTION  This internship represented an important stage in both my professional and personal  development.

It allowed me to understand my own value with greater clarity and to identify  more precisely my strengths, weaknesses, and distinguishing qualities. One of the main  lessons obtained from this experience was the confirmation that I work best when I  understand a process in depth. I tend to analyze what I am doing carefully, investigate the  technical basis of each activity, and try to anticipate the possible behavior of the system.

This approach not only improved my own performance, but also helped build trust in the  work assigned to me. At the same time, the experience reinforced the importance of  planning and organization as fundamental elements in engineering work. From a technical perspective, the internship allowed me to strengthen my understanding of  chemistry, process behavior, and microbiological systems under real laboratory conditions.

My contribution to the laboratory was mainly based on daily support, careful execution of  assigned tasks, and responsible handling of samples and data. I aimed to perform each  activity with concentration, consistency, and the lowest possible level of experimental error. In return, the laboratory provided an environment in which learning was progressive and well  supported.

Working with experienced and collaborative people helped me clarify doubts,  improve my technical judgment, and better understand the human side of professional work. This experience also helped me mature, especially by understanding that mistakes are part  of the learning process and that they should be approached with responsibility and a  willingness to improve.

After this internship, I continue the next stage of my academic path  with greater confidence, stronger motivation, and a clearer understanding of my professional  interests. 15   7. embeddings_data.texto: ABSTRACT  G-Tech Aguas Laboratory, part of the Green Technologies Research Group at Universidad  de los Andes, develops research activities focused on water treatment, water  characterization, and microbiological processes for contaminant removal. During the  pre-professional internship, work was carried out in two main areas.

The first was the  pilot-scale implementation of an electrocoagulation system for brine treatment, which  included support in hydraulic reconditioning, system layout, preparation of electrodes, and  testing of the module prior to operation. The second was the operation and monitoring of two  biological reactors for nitrogen compound removal, including sampling, physicochemical  analyses, data recording, and interpretation of reactor behavior.

The most relevant challenge  was the conditioning of the pilot-scale module, since it required understanding the hydraulic  sequence of the system, solving operational problems, and supporting technical decisions  needed to make the unit functional. The internship provided important learning in process  analysis, laboratory work, planning, and engineering criteria.
It also strengthened technical  confidence, discipline in data handling, and the ability to understand and evaluate real  treatment systems under experimental conditions. 1   INDEX  1.

INTRODUCTION  The pre-professional internship was carried out from December 8, 2025, to March 5, 2026,  at G-Tech Aguas Laboratory, located at Universidad de los Andes and linked to the Faculty  of Engineering. The work was developed in a research environment focused on water  treatment and characterization.

The internship included activities related to pilot-scale brine  treatment through alternating current electrocoagulation, a technique commonly applied in  water and wastewater treatment (Mollah et al., 2001), and the monitoring of biological  reactors for nitrogen compound removal.

1.1 COMPANY DESCRIPTION  G-Tech Aguas Laboratory was part of the Green Technologies Research Group at  Universidad de los Andes and was linked to the Faculty of Engineering. The water treatment  line led by Professor César Huilinir started in 2024, when the first activities were still carried  out in the original G-Tech laboratory, located on the second floor of the Engineering  Laboratory building. In March 2025, the new physical space was officially opened next to the  Civil Works laboratory.

From that moment, the work was organized in two adjacent sections:  G-Tech Aguas and G-Tech Gases. As shown in Figure 1, G-Tech Aguas Laboratory operated in close connection with the  original G-Tech laboratory and with G-Tech Gases. During the internship period, the  laboratory had around eleven active members, including two professors, one laboratory  coordinator, three PhD students, one thesis student, and five interns.

The original G-Tech  laboratory was mainly oriented toward bioprocesses, biological engineering, and chemical  reactor studies, while G-Tech Aguas focused on water treatment and water characterization. G-Tech Gases was focused on projects involving microorganisms and gas-operated  reactors. Although each laboratory developed its own projects autonomously, technical  support was shared whenever needed.

The laboratory also continued to receive new  students through internships, thesis work, and graduate research activities. Figure 1. Diagram institutional location of G-Tech Aguas Laboratory. Source: Author’s own elaboration. 3.

The activities performed in this area included pilot-scale system  implementation, hydraulic assembly support, sample collection, physicochemical analysis,  biological reactor monitoring, culture media preparation, and data recording.

The main  responsibilities assigned during the internship were support in the implementation of a  pilot-scale electrocoagulation system for brine treatment and, later, the monitoring, control,  and characterization of two biological reactors for nitrogen compound removal. The closest working  relationship was maintained with Cristóbal Gallardo, especially during the implementation  and operation of the pilot-scale system. During the internship, the laboratory team also  included PhD students, thesis students, and other interns working on different research  activities.

Limited but positive interaction was also maintained with nearby laboratories and  university facilities, including the original G-Tech laboratory, the Civil Works laboratory, and  other research spaces located in the same area of the campus. In the final stage of the internship, a second training  period was carried out for the operation and monitoring of two biological reactors working  with nitrifying and denitrifying microorganisms.
