During the internship period, work schedules  were flexible and depended on project needs. The laboratory coordinator usually worked  from 9:00 a.m. to 6:00 p.m., interns from 10:00 a.m. to 5:00 p.m., and professors and  graduate students attended according to the requirements of their experiments.

From an organizational point of view, this flexible structure was effective because the  laboratory worked with a small team and project-based tasks. Productivity depended mainly  on planning, experimental timing, and completion of daily objectives, which made this  dynamic appropriate for a research environment. Table 2: General Activities. Source: Author’s own elaboration. A strength of the laboratory was its collaborative environment.

Even though G-Tech Aguas,  G-Tech Gases, and the original G-Tech laboratory worked on different projects under  different coordinators and professors, mutual support was maintained whenever specific  technical questions arose. This created a favorable working environment and allowed  knowledge exchange between laboratories without affecting the autonomy of each project.

At the same time, the laboratory showed growth potential, since new thesis students and  possibly new master’s and PhD students were expected to join future projects. In that sense,  the current structure was productive and functional, although future growth could require  more formal planning of space, schedules, and equipment availability. 11  Aspect  Observation  Estimated comfortable  capacity  Around 20 people  Laboratory coordinator  schedule  9:00 a.m. – 6:00 p.m.

Intern schedule  10:00 a.m. – 5:00 p.m. Professors / graduate  students  Flexible attendance depending on project needs  Main organizational  characteristic  Flexible, project-based work dynamic  Main strength  High productivity and collaboration  Main future challenge  Space and resource planning as the group grows.

3.2 WORK ANALYSIS  The work carried out during the internship can be analyzed through two main contributions:  the pilot-scale implementation of an electrocoagulation system for brine treatment and the  monitoring of two biological reactors for nitrogen compound removal. The first contribution was related to the pilot-scale electrocoagulation module for brine  treatment.

Electrocoagulation is a treatment technique in which sacrificial metal electrodes  generate coagulant species in situ for contaminant removal from liquid streams (Mollah et  al., 2001). In this project, the treatment concept had already been validated at laboratory  scale, and the internship student joined the project during the pilot implementation stage,  when the objective was to transfer the process to a larger scale and prepare it for future  automation and possible industrial application.

The associated company was also interested  in the potential use of the recovered residues in other applications. As shown in Figure 7, the module was designed around four main hydraulic stages. First,  brine was stored in a storage tank and transported by Pump 1 to the treatment tank, where  the electrocoagulation process took place through the action of aluminum cathodes and  anodes.

The rectifier was intended to supply the electrical energy required for this stage and  to allow polarity switching and pulsed operation. After approximately 40 minutes of  treatment, the fluid was transferred by Pump 2 to the precipitation tank, where an external  flocculant was added in order to improve solid-liquid separation and reduce settling time.

Then, Pump 3 allowed the recirculation of the fluid from the precipitation tank back to the  treatment tank, which gave flexibility to the operation and made it possible to continue the  treatment cycle. Finally, Pump 4 was used to evacuate fluid from the precipitation and waste  sections toward the drain. In addition, part of the solids generated during electrocoagulation  were removed as foam, which overflowed through a bridge and was directed to the waste  tank. Figure 7.

Process diagram of the electrocoagulation module. Source: Author’s own elaboration. 12   From a work analysis perspective, the main contribution during this stage was understanding  the operating sequence of the module and supporting the hydraulic arrangement required to  make it functional at pilot scale. This included planning valves, unions, pumping lines, and  hydraulic connections so that the system could operate in a practical and maintainable way.

As described in Section 2.2, this stage also required several modifications, such as  reconditioning damaged connections and improving the discharge arrangement. Another important point was the future operation of the system. During the internship, the  rectifier had not yet arrived, so the module could not be tested under full electrocoagulation  conditions, although it was left prepared for the next commissioning stage.

This was relevant  because pulsed or alternating current can reduce electrode passivation in aluminum-based  systems (Mao et al., 2008). However, the use of aluminum electrodes also implies material  consumption and generation of metallic residues, which may become a limitation in future  automated or industrial applications. For this reason, alternative electrode materials were  already being considered. The second contribution corresponded to the monitoring of two biological reactors.

In this  project, the main objective was to evaluate whether nitrifying and denitrifying  microorganisms could sustain both processes in the same system with limited direct  aeration, using passive oxygen transfer through a perforated upper mesh (Thakur & Medhi,  2019). At the same time, the effect of zeolite was assessed by comparing a control reactor  and a reactor containing zeolite. Figure 8.

Evolution of COD in the control reactor and the zeolite reactor during a representative  operating cycle. Source: Author’s own elaboration. Samples were taken on Mondays, Wednesdays, and Fridays, with additional sampling on  Wednesdays due to media replacement. After the initial learning stage, the work focused on  routine monitoring, data recording, and analysis. The monitored variables included COD,  TAN, nitrite, nitrate, sulfate, sulfide, pH, alkalinity, and temperature.

COD was especially  relevant because it represented the organic load available in the medium, and its decrease  13   in both reactors suggested partial substrate consumption. The internship student monitored  the reactors from cycle 24 to cycle 27, and the overall behavior was similar in all of them. For  this reason, only one representative cycle is shown in the figures. Accordingly, Figure 8  should present the decrease in COD in both reactors during a representative cycle.

At the same time, the nitrogen-related variables were essential for evaluating whether  nitrification and denitrification were actually taking place under the tested conditions. TAN  decreased during the cycle, which was consistent with biological activity in the system. However, nitrite and nitrate were not clearly produced, so the expected nitrification pathway  could not be confirmed from the analytical results.

As a consequence, denitrification could  not be clearly demonstrated either, since nitrate or nitrite were not sufficiently available as  intermediates for further reduction. As in the COD analysis, the internship student worked  from cycle 24 to cycle 27 and similar trends were observed in all of them. Therefore, Figure  9 should present the evolution of TAN together with the absence or very low generation of  nitrite and nitrate in both reactors during one representative cycle. Figure 9.

TAN, nitrite, and nitrate evolution in the control reactor and the zeolite reactor during a  representative operating cycle. Source: Author’s own elaboration. By the end of February, the tested conditions did not clearly demonstrate simultaneous  nitrification and denitrification. COD and TAN decreased, indicating biological activity, but the  absence of clear nitrite and nitrate production meant that the nitrogen pathway could not be  fully confirmed.

In contrast, sulfide reduction was observed. From a work analysis  perspective, this stage was mainly based on reliable operation, routine monitoring, and  interpretation of experimental behavior. The results also suggested that a more controlled  aeration strategy could improve process predictability in future work. 14   3. PERSONAL REFLECTION  This internship represented an important stage in both my professional and personal  development.

It allowed me to understand my own value with greater clarity and to identify  more precisely my strengths, weaknesses, and distinguishing qualities. One of the main  lessons obtained from this experience was the confirmation that I work best when I  understand a process in depth. I tend to analyze what I am doing carefully, investigate the  technical basis of each activity, and try to anticipate the possible behavior of the system.

This approach not only improved my own performance, but also helped build trust in the  work assigned to me. At the same time, the experience reinforced the importance of  planning and organization as fundamental elements in engineering work. From a technical perspective, the internship allowed me to strengthen my understanding of  chemistry, process behavior, and microbiological systems under real laboratory conditions.

My contribution to the laboratory was mainly based on daily support, careful execution of  assigned tasks, and responsible handling of samples and data. I aimed to perform each  activity with concentration, consistency, and the lowest possible level of experimental error. In return, the laboratory provided an environment in which learning was progressive and well  supported.

Working with experienced and collaborative people helped me clarify doubts,  improve my technical judgment, and better understand the human side of professional work. This experience also helped me mature, especially by understanding that mistakes are part  of the learning process and that they should be approached with responsibility and a  willingness to improve.

After this internship, I continue the next stage of my academic path  with greater confidence, stronger motivation, and a clearer understanding of my professional  interests. 15   7.
